input.description: Find contributor guides in cli project

result.output: /Users/goose-guest/Documents/work/cli/docs/first-time-contributors.md

1	# First-Time Contributors
2	
3	Welcome. This guide is for people who are new to Entire, new to open source, or
4	just want a careful path through their first contribution to this repository.
5	
6	The main contribution rules live in [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md). Treat
7	that file as the source of truth. This page slows the process down and explains
8	where to start, what to say, what to run, and what to expect after you open a
9	pull request.
10	
11	## Who This Is For
12	
13	Start here if any of these are true:
14	
15	- You have never opened a pull request before.
16	- You have contributed to other projects, but not to Entire.
17	- You want help finding a contribution that is small enough to finish.
18	- You are unsure how to ask maintainers for the right amount of context.
19	
20	If you are already comfortable with this repo, use the shorter workflow in
21	[CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md).
22	
23	## Choose a First Contribution
24	
25	Start by browsing [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/entireio/cli/issues) for
26	issues that describe behavior you can reproduce, mention a command or workflow
27	you can try locally, or feel concrete enough that you can explain the problem
28	back in your own words. You can also check the `#looking-for-contributors`
29	channel in Discord. You do not need to understand the whole project before
30	asking about an issue.
31	
32	Good first contributions are small, clear, and easy to review. For this repo,
33	that usually means one of these:
34	
35	- Reproducing a bug you personally hit and adding the missing test.
36	- Clarifying a confusing CLI message, help text, or setup step.
37	- Adding a focused test around existing command behavior.
38	- Fixing a small platform-specific issue on macOS, Linux, or Windows.
39	- Improving README or contributor instructions after you hit real friction.
40	
41	When looking at an issue, favor ones where you can explain the problem back in
42	your own words. It is okay if you do not know the fix yet. If an issue is broad,
43	comment with the part you think you can tackle and ask whether that slice would
44	be useful.
45	
46	If you do not already have a specific problem in mind, ask in
47	`#looking-for-contributors` and mention what you are comfortable with, such as
48	Go tests, CLI output, setup issues, Windows behavior, or README/contributor
49	wording. A maintainer can usually point you toward a safer slice than browsing
50	alone can.
51	
52	As a first PR, avoid broad architecture changes, new agent integrations, command
53	renames, checkpoint strategy changes, or anything that changes git history
54	behavior unless a maintainer has already helped define the scope.
55	
56	## Comment Before You Start
57	
58	Before starting non-trivial code work, leave a short comment on the issue or
59	start a new issue. This helps avoid duplicated effort and gives maintainers a
60	chance to redirect you before you spend time on the wrong approach.
61	
62	A good first comment is specific:
63	
64	```text
65	Hi, I am new to Entire and would like to work on this.
66	
67	I was able to reproduce the issue by running:
68	
69	  entire status
70	
71	My plan is to update the error message in <file or command area> and add a
72	focused test for the behavior. Does that sound like the right direction?
73	```
74	
75	If you are not starting from an existing issue, open one with the smallest
76	concrete problem you can describe:
77	
78	```text
79	I hit confusing behavior while running `entire enable` on macOS.
80	
81	Expected: the command would explain which agent config was missing.
82	Actual: the command exited with "not configured".
83	
84	I can reproduce this locally. Would a small PR that improves the message and
85	adds a focused test be useful?
86	```
87	
88	You do not need a perfect plan. Showing what you have tried and where you are
89	headed is usually enough.
90	
91	## Set Up the Repository
92	
93	Fork the repository on GitHub, then clone your fork:
94	
95	```bash
96	git clone git@github.com:YOUR-USERNAME/cli.git
97	cd cli
98	```
99	
100	Add the upstream repository so you can pull in maintainer changes later:
101	
102	```bash
103	git remote add upstream git@github.com:entireio/cli.git
104	git fetch upstream
105	```
106	
107	Install the local toolchain:
108	
109	```bash
110	mise trust
111	mise install
112	go mod download
113	mise run build
114	```
115	
116	Verify the setup with the normal test suite:
117	
118	```bash
119	mise run test
120	```
121	
122	If setup fails, include the command you ran, the full error text, your OS, and
123	the output of `go version` when asking for help.
124	
125	## Create a Branch
126	
127	Create a branch from the latest `main`:
128	
129	```bash
130	git switch main
131	git pull upstream main
132	git switch -c fix/clearer-enable-error
133	```
134	
135	Use a branch name that describes the change. Examples:
136	
137	- `fix/clearer-enable-error`
138	- `test/session-status-output`
139	- `docs/setup-troubleshooting-note`
140	
141	## Make a Small Change
142	
143	Keep your first pull request narrow. A focused PR is easier to review and easier
144	to finish.
145	
146	Good first PR shapes:
147	
148	- One failing behavior plus its test.
149	- One small error message improvement.
150	- One test-only clarification.
151	- One README or setup clarification based on a real problem you hit.
152	
153	Avoid bundling unrelated cleanup with your first PR. If you notice extra things
154	while working, leave yourself a note and open a follow-up issue or PR later.
155	
156	## Use AI Carefully
157	
158	Entire CLI is not an agent. It is a CLI for capturing and navigating AI-agent
159	work, so it is completely normal to use coding agents while contributing.
160	There is no need to tell us that you used AI in your work.
161	
162	Use whatever agent and workflow you like. We hope you like Goose, of course. The
163	important rule is that you are responsible for the final code, tests, and docs.
164	Before submitting a PR, review the diff yourself. PRs that look generated,
165	unreviewed, or "vibe coded" without a human pass may be closed.
166	
167	Quick responsible AI tips:
168	
169	- **Think first.** Agents tend to jump straight to code. Ask the agent to explore
170	  the codebase first, explain the relevant architecture, and propose an approach
171	  before it edits files.
172	- **Push back on shortcuts.** Watch for trivial tests, overly wide types,
173	  optional fields added just to satisfy the compiler, swallowed errors, and
174	  copied local patterns that do not actually fit.
175	- **Notice uncertainty.** If the agent keeps declaring that it understands the
176	  issue but the patches are not converging, stop and reframe the task. Use what
177	  you learned to start a smaller attempt.
178	- **Cut the bloat.** Remove redundant comments, just-in-case logging,
179	  over-defensive branches, and tests that only check implementation details
180	  instead of user-visible behavior.
181	
182	## Run the Right Checks
183	
184	For wording-only changes, run a lightweight sanity check:
185	
186	```bash
187	git diff --check
188	```
189	
190	For Go code changes, run:
191	
192	```bash
193	mise run fmt
194	mise run lint
195	mise run test
196	```
197	
198	Before a larger PR or when a maintainer asks for full verification, run:
199	
200	```bash
201	mise run check
202	```
203	
204	Do not run real agent E2E tests unless a maintainer explicitly asks you to. They
205	can make real agent API calls. The deterministic canary E2E suite is safe to run
206	when your change touches E2E prompt wording or test infrastructure:
207	
208	```bash
209	mise run test:e2e:canary
210	```
211	
212	## Commit Your Work
213	
214	Commit with a short, descriptive message:
215	
216	```bash
217	git add <files you changed>
218	git commit -m "Clarify enable error when agent config is missing"
219	```
220	
221	Entire is used to dogfood Entire. If you have the Entire CLI installed and
222	enabled in this repository, the git hook may add an `Entire-Checkpoint` trailer
223	to your commit message automatically. That is expected. See
224	[Using Entire While Contributing](../CONTRIBUTING.md#using-entire-while-contributing)
225	for the full details.
226	
227	## Open a Pull Request
228	
229	Push your branch to your fork:
230	
231	```bash
232	git push -u origin fix/clearer-enable-error
233	```
234	
235	Then open a pull request against `entireio/cli` on GitHub.
236	
237	In the PR description, include:
238	
239	- The issue it addresses, if there is one.
240	- A short summary of what changed.
241	- The checks you ran, such as `mise run test` or `git diff --check`.
242	- Anything you want reviewers to pay special attention to.
243	
244	If your PR is not ready for final review yet, open it as a draft. Draft PRs are
245	useful when you want early feedback on direction.
246	
247	## Respond to Review
248	
249	Review is part of the contribution, not a sign that you did something wrong.
250	Entire PRs may receive automated Copilot comments and maintainer comments.
251	
252	For each review comment:
253	
254	- If it is right, push a follow-up commit that addresses it.
255	- If you are unsure, ask a question on the PR.
256	- If you disagree, explain your reasoning briefly and respectfully.
257	
258	After pushing updates, leave a short comment saying what changed. GitHub does
259	not always make it obvious which comments were addressed by a new commit.
260	
261	## If You Get Stuck
262	
263	Ask early, and include context. Good help requests include:
264	
265	- What you are trying to do.
266	- The command you ran.
267	- The exact error or output.
268	- What you already checked.
269	- Your OS and Go version when setup or tests are involved.
270	
271	Useful places to ask:
272	
273	- The GitHub issue you are working on.
274	- The pull request, if one is already open.
275	- [Discord](https://discord.gg/jZJs3Tue4S) for general questions.
276	
277	## Quick Checklist
278	
279	Before opening your first PR:
280	
281	- You picked a small, focused change.
282	- You asked for maintainer scope before non-trivial code work.
283	- You created a branch from current `main`.
284	- You ran the checks that match your change.
285	- Your PR description explains what changed and how you tested it.
286	
287	Thank you for taking the time to contribute. Small, careful improvements make
288	the project easier for the next person too.
289

1	# Contributing to skills
2	
3	Thanks for helping improve skills. This repo is intentionally small: each
4	skill lives in `skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`, with a few plugin and extension
5	metadata files that make the same skills installable across different agents.
6	
7	## Before you start
8	
9	Fork the repo:
10	
11	1. Click [**Fork**](https://github.com/entireio/skills/fork).
12	2. Clone your fork:
13	
14	```bash
15	git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/skills.git
16	cd skills
17	```
18	
19	Create a descriptive branch from `main`. Use a name that explains the skill or
20	change you are working on:
21	
22	```bash
23	git checkout main
24	git pull origin main
25	git checkout -b add-session-review-skill
26	```
27	
28	## Adding or changing a skill
29	
30	Each skill must live at:
31	
32	```text
33	skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
34	```
35	
36	Every `SKILL.md` needs YAML front matter with at least:
37	
38	```yaml
39	---
40	name: skill-name
41	description: A short description of when an agent should use this skill.
42	---
43	```
44	
45	The `name` and `description` fields are required for skill discovery. Missing
46	metadata can cause installers to skip the skill.
47	
48	Before writing, read one existing skill that is similar to your change. For
49	example, if you are adding a provenance skill, read `skills/explain/SKILL.md` or
50	`skills/what-happened/SKILL.md` first so the new instructions match the repo's
51	style and level of detail.
52	
53	Good skill instructions should:
54	
55	- say when to use the skill and when not to use it
56	- give the agent a concrete workflow
57	- name required commands, files, and expected inputs
58	- describe failure modes and what the agent should tell the user
59	- avoid secrets, private transcripts, or user-specific local paths
60	- stay focused on one repeatable workflow instead of becoming a general manual
61	
62	If you add, remove, rename, or materially change a skill, update `README.md`
63	when the public list or examples change.
64	
65	## Validation
66	
67	Before opening a PR, check that the skills installer can discover the repo
68	skills:
69	
70	```bash
71	npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes
72	```
73	
74	If you changed JSON metadata, validate the JSON:
75	
76	```bash
77	node -e 'for (const f of ["package.json","gemini-extension.json",".codex-plugin/plugin.json",".claude-plugin/plugin.json",".cursor-plugin/plugin.json",".agents/plugins/marketplace.json"]) JSON.parse(require("fs").readFileSync(f, "utf8"))'
78	```
79	
80	If you changed the OpenCode plugin shim, check its syntax:
81	
82	```bash
83	node --check .opencode/plugins/entire.js
84	```
85	
86	For Markdown-only changes, read the rendered Markdown or preview it in GitHub
87	before submitting.
88	
89	## Pull requests
90	
91	Push your branch:
92	
93	```bash
94	git push origin add-session-review-skill
95	```
96	
97	Open a pull request:
98	
99	1. Go to your fork on GitHub.
100	2. Click **Compare & pull request**.
101	3. Make sure the base repository is `entireio/skills` and the base branch is
102	   `main`.
103	
104	In the PR description, include:
105	
106	- what changed
107	- why it changed
108	- which skill changed, if any
109	- what validation you ran
110	
111	If you changed skill behavior, include a short example prompt that demonstrates
112	the new or updated behavior.
113	
114	## Support
115	
116	For bugs, feature requests, or questions, open a
117	[GitHub issue](https://github.com/entireio/skills/issues). You can also join the
118	[Entire Community Discord Server](https://discord.gg/jZJs3Tue4S).
119	
120	## Style
121	
122	- Prefer concise, direct instructions over long explanations.
123	- Use exact commands when the workflow depends on a command.
124	- Keep examples generic unless a specific tool or repo is required.
125	- Do not paste raw session transcripts into skills.
126	- Do not commit local agent state, logs, temp files, or machine-specific config.
127

1	![skills cover](assets/gh-repo-cover.png)
2	
3	# skills
4	
5	Bring your Entire context to your agents with cross-agent skills.
6	
7	## Why This Exists
8	
9	The [Entire CLI](https://github.com/entireio/cli) captures the context behind your
10	code changes: prompts, transcripts,
11	[Checkpoints](https://docs.entire.io/cli/checkpoints), and the decisions that led
12	to each change, alongside your git history. This repository packages
13	agent-invokable workflows that teach coding agents how to use that context across
14	development environments.
15	
16	Instead of looking up Entire commands yourself, you can ask in plain language and
17	let your agent search prior work, inspect provenance, hand off session state, or
18	turn repeated workflows into new skills.
19	
20	## Install
21	
22	Install every skill with the [skills](https://skills.sh/) CLI:
23	
24	```bash
25	npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills --all
26	```
27	
28	Install one skill:
29	
30	```bash
31	npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills --skill search
32	```
33	
34	See [Agent-Specific Installation](#agent-specific-installation) for setup
35	instructions by agent.
36	
37	## Quick Start
38	
39	> [!NOTE]
40	> These skills are most useful in codebases with real
41	> [Checkpoints](https://docs.entire.io/cli/checkpoints) and session history. The
42	> richer the history, the more context your agent has to work with; in a new or
43	> lightly captured repository, some workflows may have less to find or explain.
44	
45	After installing, ask your agent for the workflow you want:
46	
47	```text
48	search past work for rate limiting
49	```
50	
51	```text
52	explain src/auth.ts
53	```
54	
55	```text
56	what happened here: src/auth.ts:42-57
57	```
58	
59	```text
60	hand off this session
61	```
62	
63	```text
64	turn my release notes workflow into a skill
65	```
66	
67	For a guided walkthrough, see the
68	[skills tutorial](https://docs.entire.io/skills/tutorial).
69	
70	## What skills help agents do
71	
72	| Goal | Example prompt |
73	| --- | --- |
74	| Find prior work before making changes | `search past work for the migration` |
75	| Understand the intent behind a function, file, or line | `explain parseConfig` |
76	| Investigate the latest change to a specific block | `what happened at src/auth.ts:42` |
77	| Pick up another agent's work | `hand off the codex session` |
78	| Convert repeated work into a reusable workflow | `make a skill from this session` |
79	
80	## Included skills
81	
82	The current repository includes these skills. Each skill lives in
83	`skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`.
84	
85	### `search`
86	
87	Finds prior work in your Entire history by topic, repo, branch, author, or time
88	window, so your agent can bring past context into the current task before making
89	changes.
90	
91	https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/52e50eae-ba53-4f31-ad2e-2a29a767a34f
92	
93	### `explain`
94	
95	Looks up the session behind a function, file, or line so your agent can explain
96	the requirement, decision, or original problem that shaped it.
97	
98	https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ab2e88f6-9bce-417b-ba71-6e93ecad370b
99	
100	### `what-happened`
101	
102	Starts from an exact file line, range, or pasted snippet and traces the latest
103	change with `git blame` and Checkpoint context. Useful when reviewing a concrete
104	block, debugging a regression, or asking why that block changed.
105	
106	https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/47db0daa-60bd-4000-b956-2d0e4d980b9b
107	
108	### `session-handoff`
109	
110	Reads saved or active session context so another agent can pick up the task
111	state, important discoveries, blockers, and next steps without making you
112	reconstruct everything manually.
113	
114	https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0df3b5cd-fe37-4145-af48-138642ccc8bc
115	
116	## Requirements
117	
118	These skills are designed for repositories where Entire has captured useful
119	history. Some workflows need:
120	
121	- the [Entire CLI](https://github.com/entireio/cli) installed
122	- a git repository with Entire sessions, Checkpoints, or Checkpoint-backed commits
123	- [`entire login`](https://docs.entire.io/cli/commands#login) for workflows that
124	  search indexed history
125	- a GitHub `origin` and pushed, indexed Checkpoints for remote search results
126	
127	Individual skills handle missing context differently and should explain what is
128	missing before falling back or stopping.
129	
130	## Agent-Specific Installation
131	
132	<!-- prettier-ignore-start -->
133	
134	<details>
135	<summary>Claude Code</summary>
136	
137	```bash
138	/plugin marketplace add entireio/skills
139	/plugin install entire
140	```
141	
142	</details>
143	
144	<details>
145	<summary>Codex (OpenAI)</summary>
146	
147	Clone into the cross-client discovery path:
148	
149	```bash
150	git clone https://github.com/entireio/skills.git ~/.agents/skills/entire
151	```
152	
153	Codex auto-discovers skills from `~/.agents/skills/` and `.agents/skills/`.
154	Update with:
155	
156	```bash
157	cd ~/.agents/skills/entire && git pull
158	```
159	
160	</details>
161	
162	<details>
163	<summary>Cursor</summary>
164	
165	Copy skills into the cross-client discovery directory:
166	
167	```bash
168	git clone https://github.com/entireio/skills.git ~/.cursor/skills/entire
169	```
170	
171	Cursor auto-discovers skills from `.agents/skills/` and `.cursor/skills/`.
172	
173	</details>
174	
175	<details>
176	<summary>Copilot</summary>
177	
178	```bash
179	/plugin install https://github.com/entireio/skills
180	# or
181	git clone https://github.com/entireio/skills.git ~/.copilot/skills/entire
182	```
183	
184	Copilot auto-discovers skills from `.copilot/skills/`.
185	
186	</details>
187	
188	<details>
189	<summary>Gemini CLI</summary>
190	
191	```bash
192	gemini extensions install https://github.com/entireio/skills
193	```
194	
195	Update with:
196	
197	```bash
198	gemini extensions update entire
199	```
200	
201	</details>
202	
203	<details>
204	<summary>OpenCode</summary>
205	
206	Copy skills into the cross-client discovery directory:
207	
208	```bash
209	git clone https://github.com/entireio/skills.git ~/.agents/skills/entire
210	```
211	
212	OpenCode auto-discovers skills from `.agents/skills/`, `.opencode/skills/`, and
213	`.claude/skills/`.
214	
215	</details>
216	
217	<!-- prettier-ignore-end -->
218	
219	## Contributing
220	
221	See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for local development, validation, and pull
222	request guidance. Please also follow the
223	[Code of Conduct](https://github.com/entireio/.github/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md).
224	
225	## Support
226	
227	For bugs, feature requests, or questions, open a
228	[GitHub issue](https://github.com/entireio/skills/issues). You can also join the
229	[Entire Community Discord Server](https://discord.gg/jZJs3Tue4S).
230	
231	## License
232	
233	This project is licensed under the terms in [LICENSE](LICENSE).
234

content.text: I have enough context. Let me check if a `docs/` folder exists already and verify a couple of details before drafting.

result.output:
explain
search
session-handoff
session-to-skill
what-happened
(Bash completed with no output)

content.text: No `docs/` folder yet. I'll create the guide there (matching the cli layout) and link it from CONTRIBUTING.md. Here's what I'll create — let me know if you want any sections different.

input.content:
# First-Time Contributors

Welcome. This guide is for people who are new to Entire, new to open source, or
just want a careful path through their first contribution to this repository.

The main contribution rules live in [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md). Treat
that file as the source of truth. This page slows the process down and explains
where to start, what to say, what to run, and what to expect after you open a
pull request.

## Who This Is For

Start here if any of these are true:

- You have never opened a pull request before.
- You have contributed to other projects, but not to Entire.
- You want help finding a contribution that is small enough to finish.
- You are unsure how to ask maintainers for the right amount of context.

If you are already comfortable with this repo, use the shorter workflow in
[CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md).

## What This Repo Contains

This repo is intentionally small. Each skill lives in
`skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`, with a few plugin and extension metadata files
that make the same skills installable across different agents (Claude Code,
Codex, Cursor, Copilot, Gemini, OpenCode).

A skill is a short Markdown file with YAML front matter that tells a coding
agent when to run a specific workflow and how to run it. Most contributions are
edits to a `SKILL.md` file, a small new skill, or a wording fix to the README or
contributor docs.

## Choose a First Contribution

Start by browsing [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/entireio/skills/issues) for
issues that describe behavior you can reproduce, mention a skill or prompt you
can try locally, or feel concrete enough that you can explain the problem back
in your own words. You can also ask in the
[Entire Community Discord](https://discord.gg/jZJs3Tue4S). You do not need to
understand the whole project before asking about an issue.

Good first contributions are small, clear, and easy to review. For this repo,
that usually means one of these:

- Clarifying wording in an existing `SKILL.md` after you hit real friction.
- Adding a missing failure mode or "when not to use" note to a skill.
- Improving the README or contributor instructions after you got stuck.
- Fixing a broken link, typo, or stale example command.
- Adding a small, focused new skill for a workflow you already use.

When looking at an issue, favor ones where you can explain the problem back in
your own words. It is okay if you do not know the fix yet. If an issue is broad,
comment with the part you think you can tackle and ask whether that slice would
be useful.

As a first PR, avoid sweeping rewrites of multiple skills, renaming existing
skills, or changing plugin or marketplace metadata across all the agent
integrations at once. Those changes are worth doing, but they are easier as
follow-ups once a maintainer has helped define the scope.

## Comment Before You Start

Before starting non-trivial work, leave a short comment on the issue or open a
new one. This avoids duplicated effort and gives maintainers a chance to
redirect you before you spend time on the wrong approach.

A good first comment is specific:

```text
Hi, I am new to Entire and would like to work on this.

I tried the `search` skill in my own repo and the agent could not tell whether
`entire login` was required. My plan is to update skills/search/SKILL.md so the
agent explains the missing prerequisite before falling back. Does that sound
like the right direction?
```

If you are not starting from an existing issue, open one with the smallest
concrete problem you can describe:

```text
I hit confusing behavior while asking my agent to `explain` a function.

Expected: the agent would tell me when no Entire session was found.
Actual: the agent guessed an explanation from the code only.

Would a small PR that tightens the "when not to use" section of
skills/explain/SKILL.md be useful?
```

You do not need a perfect plan. Showing what you have tried and where you are
headed is usually enough.

## Set Up the Repository

Fork the repository on GitHub, then clone your fork:

```bash
git clone git@github.com:YOUR-USERNAME/skills.git
cd skills
```

Add the upstream repository so you can pull in maintainer changes later:

```bash
git remote add upstream git@github.com:entireio/skills.git
git fetch upstream
```

There is no build step. The skills are plain Markdown files plus a small amount
of JSON metadata, so you do not need a toolchain beyond `git` and `node` (used
only for validation below).

## Create a Branch

Create a branch from the latest `main`:

```bash
git switch main
git pull upstream main
git switch -c docs/clarify-search-prereqs
```

Use a branch name that describes the change. Examples:

- `docs/clarify-search-prereqs`
- `fix/explain-when-not-to-use`
- `skill/add-release-notes`

## Make a Small Change

Keep your first pull request narrow. A focused PR is easier to review and
easier to finish.

Good first PR shapes:

- One wording fix in an existing `SKILL.md`.
- One added "when not to use" or failure-mode note.
- One small new skill for a single, repeatable workflow.
- One README or contributor doc clarification based on a real problem you hit.

If you are adding or editing a skill, read one existing skill that is similar
to your change first. For example, `skills/explain/SKILL.md` and
`skills/what-happened/SKILL.md` are good references for tone and level of
detail.

Every `SKILL.md` needs YAML front matter with at least:

```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: A short description of when an agent should use this skill.
---
```

Avoid bundling unrelated cleanup with your first PR. If you notice extra things
while working, leave yourself a note and open a follow-up issue or PR later.

## Use AI Carefully

It is completely normal to use coding agents while contributing to this repo.
We hope you like Goose, of course. There is no need to tell us that you used AI
in your work.

Use whatever agent and workflow you like. The important rule is that you are
responsible for the final wording, examples, and metadata. Before submitting a
PR, review the diff yourself. PRs that look generated, unreviewed, or "vibe
coded" without a human pass may be closed.

Quick responsible AI tips:

- **Think first.** Agents tend to jump straight to edits. Ask the agent to read
  one or two existing skills and explain the repo's style before it writes a
  new one.
- **Push back on shortcuts.** Watch for vague descriptions, hand-wavy "when to
  use" sections, and instructions that read like marketing instead of a
  concrete workflow.
- **Notice uncertainty.** If the agent keeps rewriting the same paragraph
  without converging, stop and reframe the task in fewer words.
- **Cut the bloat.** Skills should stay focused on one repeatable workflow.
  Remove general-purpose advice, unnecessary preamble, and examples that do not
  match the workflow.

## Run the Right Checks

For wording-only Markdown changes, a quick sanity check is enough:

```bash
git diff --check
```

For changes to a `SKILL.md` or new skills, confirm the installer can still
discover the skills in this repo:

```bash
npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes
```

If you changed any JSON metadata, validate that the JSON still parses:

```bash
node -e 'for (const f of ["package.json","gemini-extension.json",".codex-plugin/plugin.json",".claude-plugin/plugin.json",".cursor-plugin/plugin.json",".agents/plugins/marketplace.json"]) JSON.parse(require("fs").readFileSync(f, "utf8"))'
```

If you changed the OpenCode plugin shim, check its syntax:

```bash
node --check .opencode/plugins/entire.js
```

For Markdown-only changes, read the rendered Markdown or preview it in GitHub
before submitting.

## Commit Your Work

Commit with a short, descriptive message:

```bash
git add <files you changed>
git commit -m "Clarify search skill prerequisites"
```

If you have the Entire CLI installed and enabled in this repository, the git
hook may add an `Entire-Checkpoint` trailer to your commit message
automatically. That is expected.

## Open a Pull Request

Push your branch to your fork:

```bash
git push -u origin docs/clarify-search-prereqs
```

Then open a pull request against `entireio/skills` on GitHub.

In the PR description, include:

- The issue it addresses, if there is one.
- A short summary of what changed and why.
- Which skill changed, if any.
- The validation you ran (for example,
  `npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes`).
- If you changed skill behavior, a short example prompt that shows the new or
  updated behavior.

If your PR is not ready for final review yet, open it as a draft. Draft PRs are
useful when you want early feedback on direction.

## Respond to Review

Review is part of the contribution, not a sign that you did something wrong.

For each review comment:

- If it is right, push a follow-up commit that addresses it.
- If you are unsure, ask a question on the PR.
- If you disagree, explain your reasoning briefly and respectfully.

After pushing updates, leave a short comment saying what changed. GitHub does
not always make it obvious which comments were addressed by a new commit.

## If You Get Stuck

Ask early, and include context. Good help requests include:

- What you are trying to do.
- The skill and prompt you used.
- The exact error or unexpected output.
- What you already checked.

Useful places to ask:

- The GitHub issue you are working on.
- The pull request, if one is already open.
- [Discord](https://discord.gg/jZJs3Tue4S) for general questions.

## Quick Checklist

Before opening your first PR:

- You picked a small, focused change.
- You asked for maintainer scope before non-trivial work.
- You created a branch from current `main`.
- You ran the checks that match your change.
- Your PR description explains what changed and how you tested it.

Thank you for taking the time to contribute. Small, careful improvements make
the project easier for the next person too.

Done. Added the guide at [docs/first-time-contributors.md](docs/first-time-contributors.md), modeled on the cli version but adapted for this repo:

- No mise/Go toolchain — skills are Markdown plus a bit of JSON, so the setup section just covers `git clone` and `git remote add upstream`.
- "Good first PR shapes" are skill-specific (wording fixes in `SKILL.md`, adding "when not to use" notes, small new skills) instead of test/error-message changes.
- "Run the right checks" uses this repo's actual validation commands (`npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes`, the JSON parse one-liner, `node --check` for the OpenCode shim) instead of `mise run test`.
- Kept the structure, tone, "Use AI Carefully" section, and quick checklist from the cli version.

Also added a one-paragraph pointer near the top of [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) so newcomers can find it.

Worth a read-through to make sure the tone matches what you want — happy to tweak any section.

content.text:
Found the wrinkle: `.gitignore` has `docs` as a top-level rule (added in the very first version of `.gitignore` back in April; no motivation in the commit). The new file lives at `docs/first-time-contributors.md`, so it's currently ignored.

A few options before I commit:

1. **Remove `docs` from `.gitignore`** — cleanest if the original ignore was just preventive. Matches the cli repo (which has `docs/first-time-contributors.md` committed).
2. **Add a negation rule** like `!docs/first-time-contributors.md` — keeps `docs/` ignored for anything else (e.g. locally-generated content) but lets this file through.
3. **Move the file** to a non-ignored path, e.g. root-level `FIRST-TIME-CONTRIBUTORS.md`.

My recommendation is **option 1**: the ignore looks like a leftover, and committing under `docs/` matches the cli repo's pattern, leaving room for more docs later. Which do you want?
